compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: Companies shall integrate due diligence into all their corporate policies and risk management systems. This includes adopting a due diligence policy that describes the company's approach, code of conduct, and the processes put in place to implement due diligence, including the measures taken to verify compliance with the code of conduct and to extend its application to established business relationships.

Companies shall take appropriate measures to identify actual and potential adverse human rights impacts and adverse environmental impacts arising from their own operations or those of their subsidiaries, and from their established business relationships in their chains of activities, in accordance with the requirements of due diligence.

Companies shall take appropriate measures to prevent, or where prevention is not possible or not immediately possible, adequately mitigate potential adverse human rights impacts and adverse environmental impacts that have been, or should have been, identified pursuant to Article 6, including by seeking contractual assurances from business partners.

Companies shall seek contractual assurances from a direct business partner that it will ensure compliance with the company's code of conduct and, as necessary, a prevention action plan, including by seeking corresponding contractual assurances from its partners, to the extent that their activities are part of the company's chain of activities (contractual cascading).

Companies shall make necessary investments, such as into management or production processes and infrastructures, to comply with their due diligence obligations, and shall provide targeted and proportionate support for SMEs with which they have an established business relationship, where compliance with the code of conduct or the prevention action plan would jeopardise the viability of the SME.

Companies shall take appropriate measures to bring actual adverse impacts to an end. Where the adverse impact cannot be brought to an end, the company shall minimise the extent of such impact. The company shall seek contractual assurances from a direct business partner for remediation and shall cascade these obligations through the value chain.

Where the adverse impact cannot be brought to an end or adequately mitigated, the company shall refrain from entering into new or extending existing relations with the partner in connection with or in the chain of activities of which the impact has arisen, and where the law governing their relations so entitles them, shall take the necessary steps to temporarily suspend or terminate the business relationship.

Companies shall provide the possibility for persons affected by adverse impacts, trade unions, and civil society organisations to submit complaints to the company where they have legitimate concerns regarding actual or potential adverse human rights or environmental impacts. Companies shall establish a fair, publicly available, accessible, predictable, and transparent complaints procedure.

Companies shall carry out periodic assessments of their own operations and measures, those of their subsidiaries and, where related to the chains of activities of the company, those of their established business relationships, to monitor the effectiveness of the identification, prevention, mitigation, bringing to an end, and minimisation of the extent of human rights and environmental adverse impacts.

Companies shall publish an annual statement on their website describing the due diligence processes implemented, the actual and potential adverse impacts identified, the actions taken to prevent, mitigate, bring to an end, or minimise those impacts, and the outcomes of those actions. The statement must cover the company's own operations, those of its subsidiaries, and its established business relationships.

Companies shall adopt and put into effect a transition plan for climate change mitigation which aims to ensure, through best efforts, that the business model and strategy of the company are compatible with the transition to a sustainable economy and with the limiting of global warming to 1.5C in line with the Paris Agreement, including its intermediate and 2050 climate neutrality targets.

Member States shall ensure that companies are liable for damages to natural or legal persons if the company intentionally or negligently failed to comply with its obligations under Articles 7 and 8 and as a result of such failure an adverse impact that should have been identified, prevented, mitigated, brought to an end, or its extent minimised resulted in damage to that person's legal interest.

Each Member State shall designate one or more supervisory authorities responsible for supervising compliance with the obligations laid down in the Directive. Supervisory authorities shall have the power to carry out investigations, order companies to cease infringements, and impose pecuniary sanctions. Maximum penalties shall be not less than 5% of worldwide net turnover.

The Annex lists specific environmental adverse impacts that fall within the scope of due diligence obligations, including violations of the Minamata Convention on Mercury, the Stockholm Convention on Persistent Organic Pollutants, the Basel Convention on Hazardous Wastes, and the Convention on Biological Diversity. Contracts must require compliance with these environmental standards throughout the supply chain.

The Annex lists specific human rights adverse impacts that trigger due diligence obligations, including violations of ILO core conventions on forced labour (No. 29 and 105), child labour (No. 138 and 182), discrimination (No. 111), equal remuneration (No. 100), freedom of association (No. 87), and right to organise and collective bargaining (No. 98). Contractual clauses must prohibit these violations and require cascading to sub-suppliers.

Relevant commodities and products (cattle, cocoa, coffee, oil palm, rubber, soya, wood and derived products) shall not be placed on, made available on, or exported from the Union market unless they are deforestation-free, have been produced in accordance with the relevant legislation of the country of production, and are covered by a due diligence statement.

Operators shall exercise due diligence for each relevant commodity and product placed on, made available on, or exported from the Union market. The due diligence system shall contain information collection, risk assessment, and risk mitigation procedures. The operator must collect information including geolocation coordinates of all plots of land where the relevant commodities were produced.

Operators shall collect the geolocation coordinates of all plots of land where the relevant commodities that the relevant product contains or has been made using were produced, together with the date or time range of production, the quantity, the country of production, and adequate and verifiable information that the products are deforestation-free and have been produced in accordance with the relevant legislation of the country of production.

Operators shall assess the risk that relevant commodities and products intended to be placed on, made available on, or exported from the Union market are non-compliant. The risk assessment shall take into account the country benchmarking system, the complexity of the supply chain, the risk of circumvention, and the risk of mixing with products of unknown origin.

Where the risk assessment reveals a non-negligible risk, operators shall carry out risk mitigation procedures before placing relevant commodities or products on the Union market. Risk mitigation may include requiring additional information, data, or documents from suppliers, carrying out independent surveys or audits, or other measures adequate to reach a conclusion of no or negligible risk.

The Commission shall classify countries or parts thereof as low, standard, or high risk based on criteria including rate of deforestation and forest degradation, rate of expansion of agricultural land, production trends for relevant commodities, and information from indigenous peoples and local communities. High-risk classifications trigger enhanced due diligence for operators sourcing from those regions.

Member States shall lay down rules on penalties applicable to infringements of the Regulation. Penalties shall include fines proportionate to the environmental damage and the value of the relevant commodities or products, confiscation of the relevant products and revenues, exclusion from public procurement processes for up to 12 months, and temporary prohibition on placing products on the market.

Traders who are not SMEs shall be subject to the same obligations as operators, including the requirement to exercise due diligence, collect geolocation data, perform risk assessments, and submit due diligence statements before making relevant commodities or products available on the Union market. Contracts with downstream traders must include representations regarding deforestation-free status.

A commercial organisation that supplies goods or services and has an annual turnover of not less than GBP 36 million must prepare a slavery and human trafficking statement for each financial year. The statement must set out the steps the organisation has taken during the financial year to ensure that slavery and human trafficking is not taking place in any of its supply chains or in any part of its own business, or state that it has taken no such steps.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: The Home Office published a list of non-compliant organisations in 2021. Multiple companies including Boohoo faced investor action following investigative journalism revealing supply chain labour abuses.

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: The slavery and human trafficking statement may include information about the organisation's structure, its business, and its supply chains. Contracts should require suppliers to disclose their organisational structure, the countries they operate in, the nature of their supply chains, and any areas of the business or supply chains where there is a risk of slavery and human trafficking.

The statement may include the organisation's policies in relation to slavery and human trafficking. Contracts should require suppliers to adopt and maintain anti-slavery policies, communicate those policies to all employees and sub-contractors, and provide evidence of policy implementation and training.

The statement may include the organisation's due diligence processes in relation to slavery and human trafficking in its business and supply chains. Contracts should require suppliers to implement due diligence processes including risk assessments, supplier audits, worker interviews, and grievance mechanisms proportionate to the risk profile of their operations and supply chain.

The statement may include the parts of the organisation's business and supply chains where there is a risk of slavery and human trafficking taking place, and the steps it has taken to assess and manage that risk. Contracts should require suppliers to identify high-risk geographies, sectors, and supplier tiers, and implement enhanced due diligence for those areas.

The statement may include the organisation's effectiveness in ensuring that slavery and human trafficking is not taking place in its business or supply chains, measured against such performance indicators as it considers appropriate. Contracts should define KPIs for anti-slavery performance, such as percentage of suppliers audited, number of remediation actions completed, and worker satisfaction scores.

The statement may include the training about slavery and human trafficking available to the organisation's staff. Contracts should require suppliers to provide regular training to procurement staff, management, and workers on identifying indicators of modern slavery, reporting mechanisms, and the legal obligations under the Modern Slavery Act.

A person commits the offence of slavery, servitude, and forced or compulsory labour if the person holds another person in slavery or servitude, or requires another person to perform forced or compulsory labour. Contracts must include warranties that neither the supplier nor any entity in its supply chain engages in conduct constituting an offence under this section, with immediate termination rights upon breach.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: R v Rooney and others [2017]: traffickers convicted under s.1-2 for labour exploitation in the construction sector, receiving sentences of 5-11 years.

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: A person commits the offence of human trafficking if the person arranges or facilitates the travel of another person with a view to that person being exploited. Contracts should include representations that the supplier does not engage in or facilitate human trafficking and requires the same of its supply chain, including prohibition on confiscation of identity documents and charging of recruitment fees to workers.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: R v Zielinski and others [2017]: gang convicted of human trafficking for labour exploitation in agriculture and food processing.

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: The court may make a slavery and trafficking prevention order where a person has been convicted of a slavery or human trafficking offence, or where the court is satisfied that there is a risk that the person may commit a slavery or human trafficking offence and the order is necessary to protect persons from the physical or psychological harms which would be likely to occur. Supply chain contracts should address the consequences of a supplier or its principals being subject to such an order.

Companies shall set up an adequate and effective risk management system to comply with their due diligence obligations. The risk management system shall be anchored in all relevant business processes, and the company shall designate a responsible person or persons within the company to monitor the risk management (human rights officer).

Companies shall carry out an appropriate risk analysis to determine human rights and environmental risks in their own business area and at their direct suppliers. The risk analysis must be carried out once a year and on an ad hoc basis when the company must expect a significantly changed or significantly expanded risk situation. Contractual obligations should require suppliers to participate in and provide information for risk assessments.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: BAFA initiated proceedings against a German automotive company in 2024 for inadequate risk analysis of tier-1 suppliers in Southeast Asia.

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: Companies shall take appropriate preventive measures if the risk analysis reveals risks. These include developing and implementing a human rights strategy, adapting procurement practices, conducting training, and implementing risk-based control measures. Contractual clauses must require suppliers to implement appropriate preventive measures and to contractually assure compliance with the company's human rights expectations.

Companies shall take remedial action without undue delay if they identify that a human rights or environmental violation has occurred or is imminent in their own business area or at a direct supplier. Remedial measures must include developing and implementing a remediation concept, potentially including cessation of the business relationship if other measures are ineffective. Contracts must define remediation procedures, timelines, and escalation mechanisms.

Companies shall establish an accessible complaints procedure enabling persons to draw attention to human rights and environmental risks and violations of human rights-related or environmental obligations resulting from the economic activities of a company in its own business area or from those of a direct supplier. The procedure must be publicly accessible, confidential, and impartial. Supply chain contracts should require suppliers to maintain or participate in grievance mechanisms.

Companies shall prepare an annual report on the fulfilment of their due diligence obligations in the previous financial year. The report shall be made publicly available on the company's website free of charge for a period of seven years. The report must describe the identified risks, the measures taken, the company's assessment of the impact and effectiveness of the measures, and the conclusions drawn for future measures. Contracts must support the company's ability to report by requiring supplier cooperation and data sharing.

If a company obtains substantiated knowledge of a possible violation of a human rights-related or environmental obligation at an indirect supplier, it must without undue delay carry out a risk analysis, take appropriate preventive measures, and create and implement a concept for prevention or minimisation. Due diligence obligations extend beyond direct suppliers when there are concrete indications of abuse. Contracts should include flow-down clauses requiring direct suppliers to impose equivalent obligations on their sub-suppliers.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: BAFA indicated in 2024 guidance that companies must respond to credible NGO reports about indirect supplier violations within a reasonable time frame.

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: Violations of the due diligence obligations under the Act may result in fines of up to EUR 8 million or up to 2% of the average annual worldwide turnover for companies with turnover exceeding EUR 400 million. Companies may also be excluded from public procurement for up to three years. Contracts should address allocation of liability for fines arising from supplier non-compliance with due diligence obligations.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: BAFA issued multiple warnings and initiated formal proceedings against companies in 2024, with specific focus on inadequate risk analysis and complaints mechanisms.
